era_context: The Carolingian Empire under Louis the Pious is approaching its final years. The Abbasid Caliphate under al-Mu'tasim is consolidating military power through the use of slave soldiers. The Byzantine Empire under Theophilus is engaged in military campaigns against the Abbasid Caliphate. The Tang Dynasty in China is in decline, with regional fragmentation accelerating. Viking raids on Western Europe are intensifying.

events.title: Battle of Amorium (Byzantine-Abbasid conflict)
events.description: The Byzantine Emperor Theophilus leads a military campaign into Abbasid territory in Anatolia. The campaign culminates in the Battle of Amorium (also spelled Amuriyya), where Byzantine forces are decisively defeated by the Abbasid army under the command of al-Mu'tasim and his Turkish general Ashinas. The Byzantine defeat is a major setback for Byzantine military prestige and leads to increased Abbasid raids into Byzantine territory. The battle marks a turning point in Byzantine-Abbasid relations, with the Abbasid Caliphate gaining the military initiative. Theophilus is forced to pay tribute and ransom prisoners.

sources.name: Tabari's History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Amorium is well-documented in both Byzantine and Islamic sources. The date of 838 is accepted by most modern scholars, though some sources place it in 839. The battle's significance is undisputed.
cross_references: Part of broader Byzantine-Abbasid frontier conflicts (820s–860s)
Related to 839-001 (continuation of Byzantine-Abbasid military campaign)

events.title: Frankish civil unrest and regional fragmentation
events.description: The Frankish Empire under Louis the Pious continues to experience internal tensions and regional fragmentation. Magnates and regional lords are consolidating power, and the emperor's authority is weakening. The succession dispute between Louis's sons is becoming increasingly visible. Regional assemblies (placita) are being dominated by local powers rather than imperial authority. This year marks a continuation of the political instability that will culminate in Louis's death in 840 and the subsequent civil war.
events.certainty_note: The general pattern of Frankish political instability in 838 is documented in contemporary annals. Specific events are sometimes difficult to date precisely.
cross_references: Part of broader Carolingian succession crisis (830s–840s)

events.title: Viking raid on Nantes and Loire valley
events.description: Norse raiders conduct a significant raid on Nantes and the Loire valley in western Francia. The raid is part of the intensifying Viking expansion into Western Europe during the 830s–840s. The weakness of Frankish central authority following Louis the Pious's declining health makes coordinated defense difficult. These raids presage the eventual establishment of Viking settlements in Normandy and the Loire valley.
events.certainty_note: Viking raids on the Loire valley are documented in Frankish annals for the 830s–840s period. The specific year 838 is consistent with documented patterns, though exact dating of individual raids is sometimes approximate.
cross_references: Related to 840-003 (Viking raids on Seine valley)

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Amorium's significance as a 'turning point' in Byzantine-Abbasid relations has been somewhat revised by modern scholarship. Some historians argue that the battle was primarily a tactical victory for the Abbasids rather than a strategic watershed, and that Byzantine military power remained substantial. Additionally, the notion that the battle 'ended' Byzantine military dominance in Anatolia is an oversimplification; Byzantine forces continued to conduct campaigns in the region for decades afterward. The battle's symbolic significance may have been greater than its actual military consequences.
historiographic_note: The year 838 is well-documented for Western Europe and the Byzantine-Islamic frontier, but documentation is heavily weighted toward military and political events. The Frankish annals and Byzantine chronicles are our primary sources, and they reflect the perspectives of clerical and court historians. Islamic sources are later compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Asia remain undocumented for this year. The 'Western European perspective' dominates surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Amorium led to continued Byzantine-Abbasid military campaigns in 839
Frankish political instability in 838 contributed to the succession crisis following Louis's death in 840
Viking raids of 838 were part of the broader intensification of Norse attacks in the 830s–840s
